title: OSU App Final
html:
TL;DR

  • Yahir's Oregon State application personal statement built around winning the Nick Fisher Award at YEBW summer 2019 — the formative leadership moment he keeps returning to
  • Frames the YEBW origin story: dismissed it twice over cost, then a college fair recruiter pointed him to a full-tuition scholarship he won via essay
  • Reads George Washington's biography mid-camp and uses it as the operating model: lead by surrounding-and-leveraging talent, not by being the smartest in the room
  • The Washington-as-catalyst framing becomes the through-line for Yahir's later self-image as a leader archetype
  • Multiple draft attempts visible in-doc — first time in the vault we see Yahir's revision process for a high-stakes essay

Notes

December 2020 — final-draft submission essay for Oregon State University. Prompt: an accomplishment that sparked personal growth. Answer: Young Entrepreneurs Business Week 2019 and the Nick Fisher Award.

The narrative arc: an ambitious immigrant-raised boy from a small town (Grants Pass) walks into a camp full of academically-accelerated private-school kids and finds his "value" is not knowing more, but creating "an aura of collaboration." He pulls a fractured 12-person company together by tossing out a full work afternoon for a games-based bonding session, then carries the presentation. They take 2nd; he wins the camp's top individual honor (Nick Fisher Award) — judged across 150+ students.

The Washington biography ("Indispensable Man") shows up here for the first time as Yahir's leadership reading foundation: "Washington was neither the strongest soldier nor the brightest man, but [his great trait was] the ability to surround himself with talent." This becomes the personal manifesto Yahir cites when later describing OSIG involvement, crypto club founding, and the family-restaurant marketing pivot during COVID.

Connects to: 2021 01 26 yebw essay (sibling essay), the post OSIG validation arc.
